text: In the course of Chapter 2 , we studied the logical symbols \(\lor\) , \(\land\) and \(\exists\) , which allow complicated mathematical statements to be built up from simpler ones. For each such symbol, we learned its “grammar”: the rule for using it when it appears in a hypothesis and the rule for using it when it appears in the goal. This grammar is called natural deduction .
html: In the course of Chapter 2, we studied the logical symbols \(\lor\), \(\land\) and \(\exists\), which allow complicated mathematical statements to be built up from simpler ones. For each such symbol, we learned its “grammar”: the rule for using it when it appears in a hypothesis and the rule for using it when it appears in the goal. This grammar is called natural deduction.

text: This chapter finishes the work started in Chapter 2 . We learn the grammar of the remaining logical symbols: \(\forall\) , \(\to\) , and \(\lnot\) . We also learn the grammar of two other logical symbols, \(\leftrightarrow\) and \(\exists!\) , which are less fundamental because they can be defined in terms of the other ones.
html: This chapter finishes the work started in Chapter 2. We learn the grammar of the remaining logical symbols: \(\forall\), \(\to\), and \(\lnot\). We also learn the grammar of two other logical symbols, \(\leftrightarrow\) and \(\exists!\), which are less fundamental because they can be defined in terms of the other ones.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(n\) be a natural number which is a factor of every natural number \(m\) . Show that \(n=1\) .
html: Let \(n\) be a natural number which is a factor of every natural number \(m\). Show that \(n=1\).

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: Since \(n\) is a factor of every natural number, it is a factor of \(1\) . Also notice that \(1\) is positive. So we can invoke the size bounds on factors discussed in Example 3.2.7 and Example 3.2.8 : \(n\le 1\) and \(1 \le n\) . Therefore \(n=1\) .

text: For the Lean proof, we recall that the bound from Example 3.2.7 is in Lean as Nat.le_of_dvd , and the bound from Example 3.2.8 is in Lean as Nat.pos_of_dvd_of_pos .

text: Definition
html: Definition

text: A natural number \(p\) is prime , if it is at least \(2\) , and the only factors of \(p\) are \(1\) and \(p\) .
html: A natural number \(p\) is prime, if it is at least \(2\), and the only factors of \(p\) are \(1\) and \(p\).

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that 2 is prime.
html: Show that 2 is prime.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: Clearly \(2 \le 2\) . Let \(m\) be a factor of \(2\) . Since \(2\) is positive, by the size bounds on factors discussed in Example 3.2.7 and Example 3.2.8 , we have that \(m \le 2\) and \(1 \le m\) . The only natural numbers \(m\) satisfying \(m \le 2\) and \(1 \le m\) are \(1\) and \(2\) , so as required \(m=1\) or \(m=2\) .
html: Clearly \(2 \le 2\). Let \(m\) be a factor of \(2\). Since \(2\) is positive, by the size bounds on factors discussed in Example 3.2.7 and Example 3.2.8, we have that \(m \le 2\) and \(1 \le m\). The only natural numbers \(m\) satisfying \(m \le 2\) and \(1 \le m\) are \(1\) and \(2\), so as required \(m=1\) or \(m=2\).

text: One new technique used in this solution is to observe from numeric bounds on a natural number (like \(m \le 2\) and \(1 \le m\) here) that there are finitely many possibilities (here, \(m=1\) or \(m=2\) .) In Lean, we use the tactic interval_cases for this kind of argument. It also works for integers, but it doesn’t work for rational numbers or real numbers – why?
html: One new technique used in this solution is to observe from numeric bounds on a natural number (like \(m \le 2\) and \(1 \le m\) here) that there are finitely many possibilities (here, \(m=1\) or \(m=2\).) In Lean, we use the tactic interval_cases for this kind of argument. It also works for integers, but it doesn’t work for rational numbers or real numbers – why?

html: This lemma is available for future use in Lean under the name prime_two.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that an integer \(n\) is odd, if and only if it is congruent to 1 modulo 2.
html: Show that an integer \(n\) is odd, if and only if it is congruent to 1 modulo 2.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: First, suppose that \(n\) is odd. Then there exists an integer \(k\) such that \(n=2k+1\) . Therefore \(n-1=2k\) , so \(n-1\) is divisible by 2, so \(n\equiv 1\mod 2\) .
html: First, suppose that \(n\) is odd. Then there exists an integer \(k\) such that \(n=2k+1\). Therefore \(n-1=2k\), so \(n-1\) is divisible by 2, so \(n\equiv 1\mod 2\).

text: Conversely, suppose that \(n\equiv 1\mod 2\) . Then \(2\mid n -1\) , so there exists an integer \(k\) such \(n-1=2k\) . Thus \(n=2k+1\) and so \(n\) is odd.
html: Conversely, suppose that \(n\equiv 1\mod 2\). Then \(2\mid n -1\), so there exists an integer \(k\) such \(n-1=2k\). Thus \(n=2k+1\) and so \(n\) is odd.

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: Let \(p\) be a natural number which is at least 2. Let \(T\) be another natural number, whose square is greater than \(p\) , and suppose that every natural number \(m\) for which \(1<m<T\) is not a factor of \(p\) . Then \(p\) is prime.
html: Let \(p\) be a natural number which is at least 2. Let \(T\) be another natural number, whose square is greater than \(p\), and suppose that every natural number \(m\) for which \(1<m<T\) is not a factor of \(p\). Then \(p\) is prime.

text: (Notice that in the Example 4.4.4 test we had to check that every number up to \(p\) was not a factor of \(p\) ; with this test we only need to check the numbers up to approximately the square root of \(p\) .)
html: (Notice that in the Example 4.4.4 test we had to check that every number up to \(p\) was not a factor of \(p\); with this test we only need to check the numbers up to approximately the square root of \(p\).)

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: By the prime test from Example 4.4.4 , it suffices to show that every natural number \(m\) with \(1<m<p\) is not a factor of \(p\) . Let \(m\) be such a natural number. If \(m < T\) then by hypothesis \(m\) is not a factor of \(p\) .
html: By the prime test from Example 4.4.4, it suffices to show that every natural number \(m\) with \(1<m<p\) is not a factor of \(p\). Let \(m\) be such a natural number. If \(m < T\) then by hypothesis \(m\) is not a factor of \(p\).

text: So suppose that \(T \le m\) , and that \(m\) is a factor of \(p\) . Then there exists a natural number \(l\) such that \(p= ml\) . The natural number \(l\) is a factor of \(p\) , too.
html: So suppose that \(T \le m\), and that \(m\) is a factor of \(p\). Then there exists a natural number \(l\) such that \(p= ml\). The natural number \(l\) is a factor of \(p\), too.

text: We claim that \(1<l\) . It will suffice to show that \(m \cdot 1 < ml\) , and indeed,
html: We claim that \(1<l\). It will suffice to show that \(m \cdot 1 < ml\), and indeed,

text: We also claim that \(l<T\) . It will suffice to show that \(Tl < T \cdot T\) , and indeed,
html: We also claim that \(l<T\). It will suffice to show that \(Tl < T \cdot T\), and indeed,

text: Since we have established that \(1<l<T\) , by hypothesis \(l\) is not a factor of \(p\) , contradiction. So \(m\) must not be a factor of \(p\) after all.
html: Since we have established that \(1<l<T\), by hypothesis \(l\) is not a factor of \(p\), contradiction. So \(m\) must not be a factor of \(p\) after all.
